headline: Court appoints attorney for appeal — Karmelo officially too poor to retain private counsel
body: A Collin County court has agreed to appoint an appellate attorney after Karmelo filed a declaration of indigence. Despite reports of a $625,000 GoFundMe account, the court accepted the filing. The appeal is now moving through formal channels with state-funded representation.

headline: ABC News: Legal team takes first formal step toward appeal
body: ABC News confirmed Karmelo's legal team has taken the first procedural step toward a formal appeal. The notice of appeal was filed the day after the June 9 conviction. Attorneys have identified the Batson challenge — the prosecution struck three Black jurors — as the primary appellate ground.

headline: Prosecutor breaks silence — defends verdict and jury selection process
body: Collin County First Assistant DA Bill Wirskye spoke publicly for the first time since trial, defending both the guilty verdict and the jury selection process. He pushed back on Batson challenge claims, calling the strikes race-neutral. The statement is expected to be contested at appellate level.

In his first interview after the gag order lifted, Judge John Roach Jr. — who presided over the trial — described Karmelo Anthony as someone who “seems like a nice young man.” The conviction is on appeal and the presumption of innocence governs that review; we note the presiding judge’s own on-record characterization of Karmelo’s character. — Source: Forbes, reporting Judge Roach’s on-camera remarks, June 12, 2026.
title: Even the trial judge called Karmelo “a nice young man”
Gag order lifted — and the victim's father's racially charged remarks may bear on the appeal
body: After Judge John Roach's gag order was lifted following sentencing, the victim's father gave his first unrestricted public statements — and several of them were openly racially charged toward Karmelo Anthony. Legal commentators noted the timing matters: Karmelo's appeal, filed June 10, already preserves the argument that race infected the trial process — specifically the prosecution's striking of three Black prospective jurors and the trial court's denial of the defense's Batson objection, in a county that is 12.1% Black and on a jury where not one juror was Black. Analysts observed that publicly documented racial animus surrounding a case becomes part of the record an appellate court reviews, and noted the contrast with the father's earlier statement that the case 'was never about race.' A conviction is not the final word — Karmelo retains the full constitutional right to challenge the verdict and the proceedings that produced it. Sources: Houston Public Media (NPR), June 11, 2026; KFOX-TV, June 12, 2026.

title: The Supreme Court just overturned a conviction on the same Batson grounds raised here
body: The Dallas Observer, citing Dallas appellate attorney Niles Illich, reported that under the Batson v. Kentucky precedent the United States Supreme Court ruled in favor of a death-row inmate in Mississippi in late May 2026 — overturning both his conviction and sentence — after determining a trial judge had allowed prosecutors to strike jurors based on race. That is the same legal question now preserved in Karmelo Anthony's appeal: three Black female prospective jurors were struck by prosecutors before his trial began, and the defense objected on the record, citing racial motivation. In Batson v. Kentucky (1986), the U.S. Supreme Court held that jurors may not be struck solely because of their race. A conviction is not the end of the road — Karmelo retains the full right to challenge the verdict and the proceedings that produced it. Source: Dallas Observer, June 11, 2026.

title: Appellate attorney: if the appeal turns on questions of law, 'the chance of success goes way up'
body: Dallas appellate attorney David Coale, who was not involved in the case, told CBS Texas that the strongest issues on appeal may have little to do with the evidence jurors heard and more to do with whether the trial was conducted properly. He pointed to three live questions: whether the case was tried in the right county, the race-based jury-strike (Batson) issue, and whether the jury was instructed correctly. Coale explained that appellate courts distinguish questions of fact from questions of law — and 'if the Court of Appeals sees it as questions of law, the chance of success goes way up.' These issues are now part of the record as the case moves to appeal. Source: CBS Texas, June 10, 2026.

title: Appeal may center on a Batson challenge — the State struck three Black jurors
body: KERA News, citing NBC News reporting, reported that during jury selection the State struck three potential Black jurors because they were educators. The defense objected; Judge John Roach Jr. allowed the strikes. The seated jury had no Black members in a county the U.S. Census lists as roughly 48% white. In Batson v. Kentucky, the U.S. Supreme Court held that lawyers may not use peremptory strikes solely on the basis of race. An SMU criminal-law professor told KERA that a white jury in a diverse county "is a choice — it's not inevitable." These questions are now part of the record as the case moves to appeal. Source: KERA News / NBC News, June 10, 2026.

title: Court appoints appellate counsel — the appeal is resourced and underway
body: The Dallas Morning News reported that after Karmelo Anthony filed his notice of appeal and a pauper's oath declaring indigency, the court assigned him appellate attorney Donny Perales of Chesley & Perales of McKinney. The appeal is now formally staffed. A conviction is not the end of the road — under Texas law Karmelo retains the full right to challenge the verdict and the proceedings that produced it. We do not stop until Karmelo is free. Source: The Dallas Morning News, June 10, 2026.

title: It's official: Karmelo files notice of appeal
body: One day after the jury sentenced him to 35 years — far below the up-to-99-year maximum the charge allowed — Karmelo Anthony formally filed his notice of appeal on June 10, 2026, alongside a pauper's oath requesting court-appointed appellate counsel. A conviction is not the end of the road; under Texas law Karmelo retains the full right to challenge the verdict and the proceedings that produced it. The fight moves to the appellate courts. We do not stop until Karmelo is free. Source: NBC 5 DFW, June 10, 2026.

title: Sentenced to 35 years — and the appeal begins
body: On June 9, 2026, a Collin County jury sentenced Karmelo Anthony to 35 years in prison — far below the 99-year maximum the charge allowed. A conviction is not the end of the road. Under Texas law, Karmelo retains the full right to appeal. The defense's position throughout trial never changed: prosecutors failed to prove he did anything but defend himself after he was confronted in a rival team's tent he had been invited into. That argument now becomes the foundation of the fight ahead. We do not stop until Karmelo is free. Source: NBC News / AP / Houston Public Media, June 9, 2026.

title: Defense's standing argument: 'anything but defend himself'
body: In closing arguments, defense attorney Mike Howard told jurors that the State failed to prove his client did 'anything but defend himself.' Howard established at trial that visiting rival teams' tents is customary at meets, that Karmelo was invited into the tent, and that those nearby were physically intimidating. This self-defense argument — the heart of the case — is the ground on which the appeal stands. Source: NBC News, June 9, 2026.

title: Jury composition challenged — not one juror was Black
body: Following the verdict, the Next Generation Action Network, a civil rights organization that advocated for Karmelo, denounced the racial composition of the jury, noting that not a single juror was Black in a case whose racial dimensions had been debated nationally. Questions about the fairness of the jury are part of the documented record as the case moves toward appeal. Source: NBC News, June 9, 2026.

title: Jury returns a verdict — case moves to sentencing
body: On June 9, 2026, after deliberating less than three hours, a Collin County jury found Karmelo Anthony, 19, guilty of murder in the death of Austin Metcalf at a 2025 Frisco ISD track meet. The trial moved immediately to the sentencing phase, where the jury weighs the punishment. The fight is not over: under Texas law a convicted defendant retains the right to appeal, and the sentencing range itself remains contested before the jury. Source: NBC News / CBS News Texas / WFAA / AP, June 9, 2026.

title: The 'sudden passion' question — a path to 2 to 20 years
body: In the sentencing phase, the defense asked the jury to find that the killing occurred under the immediate influence of 'sudden passion.' Under the Texas Penal Code, if jurors unanimously find sudden passion, the offense becomes a second-degree felony carrying 2 to 20 years — rather than the 5-to-99-years-or-life range. The defense argued the video showed a teenager acting in terror, without time for cool reflection. This is the live legal question now before the jury. Source: CBS News Texas, June 9, 2026.

title: 'Have mercy on my son' — a mother addresses the jury
body: As the punishment phase opened, Karmelo Anthony's mother pleaded with jurors for mercy, asking them to remember her son was a teenager at the time. Her words are part of the public record of this sentencing. Source: WFAA, June 9, 2026.

title: State's own witness: people were 'sitting all around' Karmelo — and he couldn't say it was unjustified
body: The same teammate prosecutors questioned to revisit a 'surrounded' claim also agreed on cross-examination that people were sitting all around Karmelo under the tent, that he did not see a large part of what happened, and — critically — that he did not know whether Karmelo's actions were justified. NBC News also reported the prosecution's witnesses gave conflicting accounts of the push that preceded the stabbing (described variously as a hard two-handed shove, a one-handed push, a jab, or a tap) and disagreed on whether Metcalf was joined by others in confronting Karmelo. Karmelo Anthony, 19, is presumed innocent, has pleaded not guilty, and maintains he acted in self-defense. Source: NBC News, June 8, 2026.

title: Closing arguments today — the case goes to the jury
body: With both sides rested, closing arguments are set for Tuesday and the jury will then be sequestered to deliberate. Judge Roach will determine the jury charge, which — per a court observer — may allow jurors to weigh lesser-included offenses and a self-defense justification, not only the murder charge as filed. Karmelo Anthony, 19, has pleaded not guilty and maintains he acted in self-defense. He is presumed innocent unless and until a jury finds otherwise. Source: NBC News / AP (via KSAT) / FOX 4 Dallas / WFAA, June 9, 2026.

title: Defense rests — closing arguments set for Tuesday
body: After seven days of trial, the defense rested its case Monday. Jurors were instructed to return Tuesday for closing arguments before being sequestered for deliberations. Karmelo Anthony, 19, has pleaded not guilty to the charge and maintains he acted in self-defense. He is presumed innocent. Source: Courthouse News / NBC News / FOX 4 Dallas / Dallas Morning News, June 8, 2026.

title: Detective confirms: the knife Karmelo carried was legal in Texas
body: Frisco police detective Beau Riley, called by the defense, testified that the knife Karmelo Anthony carried was legal to possess in Texas — including at stadiums. That testimony goes directly to the question of lawful conduct leading up to the confrontation. Karmelo Anthony is presumed innocent; he has pleaded not guilty and is asserting self-defense. Source: Courthouse News, June 8, 2026.

title: Defense witness: Karmelo was 'distraught' and crying afterward
body: A Centennial High School teammate who testified for the defense described Karmelo immediately after the incident as emotional and crying, comforted by a coach — 'distraught is the best way to describe it.' CNN's coverage corroborated that Anthony was 'distraught' after the confrontation. The account undercuts any portrayal of a cold or indifferent state of mind. Karmelo Anthony is presumed innocent and has pleaded not guilty. Source: Courthouse News / CNN, June 8, 2026.

title: Coach: no rule barred cross-tent mingling — the tent policy came AFTER
body: As the defense carried its case, Karmelo's coach Adam Linwood testified there was NO policy or expectation prohibiting students from sitting under other teams' tents on the day of the meet. He described high-school track culture as “organized chaos” where athletes routinely mingle with friends from other schools during downtime, and said he himself wouldn't kick another school's student out of his tent. Centennial only created a stay-under-your-own-tent rule AFTER the incident — meaning no such rule existed that day. Linwood also told jurors that Anthony earned the 2024 ‘Team Captain Award’ by a vote of his fellow players. Karmelo Anthony is presumed innocent; he has pleaded not guilty and is asserting self-defense. Source: FOX 7 Austin / FOX 4 Dallas / WFAA, June 8, 2026.

title: Texas is a stand-your-ground state — no duty to retreat
body: Legal analysts covering the trial underscore that Texas is a stand-your-ground state, meaning the central legal question is whether Karmelo was reasonably in fear — not whether he could have walked away. Under Texas law there is no duty to retreat, which is the exact framework the defense is building its case around as it presents its witnesses. The jury will be asked to weigh the situation through that self-defense standard. Karmelo Anthony is presumed innocent and has pleaded not guilty. Source: NewsNation, June 8, 2026.

title: The two were acquaintances — met repeatedly, greeted with a handshake
body: On cross-examination the defense established that the Memorial athlete who greeted Karmelo at the tent, 18-year-old Eddie Parra, was a prior acquaintance — the two had gotten together at least three times over a three-week period through a common friend — and that Parra greeted Karmelo and never personally asked him to leave. A separate defense witness recounted seeing the two shake hands, then sit and talk. That record cuts against any framing of Karmelo as a random, hostile intruder. Karmelo Anthony is presumed innocent; he has pleaded not guilty and is asserting self-defense. Source: FOX 7 Austin / FOX 4 Dallas, June 8, 2026.

title: Defense witness: the twins stood — Karmelo stayed seated
body: As the defense opened its case Saturday, a student witness testified that Austin Metcalf's twin brother, Hunter, was the first to confront Karmelo — and that BOTH twins stood while Karmelo remained seated, describing their demeanor as aggressive. The witness recalled Austin moving toward and making first physical contact with the seated 17-year-old. One seated boy, approached by two standing teens who made first contact, is the heart of the self-defense record. Notably, the State never called Hunter Metcalf to testify. Source: Dallas Morning News (Chase Rogers & Jamie Landers), June 6, 2026.

title: State's 'common denominator' witness impeached on cross
body: Defense attorney Toby Shook confronted the State's key witness — the teammate prosecutors called the 'common denominator' tying Karmelo to the tent — with photographs, text messages and social-media posts showing the two were far closer than he told the jury: parties at his home, a photo of them posing together, Karmelo playing guitar in his bedroom. A witness shown to have shaded the truth about his own relationship is a witness whose account of the words and sequence is now fairly in question. Source: Dallas Morning News, June 6, 2026.

title: Coach: cross-school mingling was routine — no rule barred it
body: A high school coach called by the defense testified that at track meets athletes from different schools routinely mingle and that he knew of NO policy preventing students from entering another team's tent area — track meets being, in his words, 'organized chaos.' This directly undercuts the framing of Karmelo as an unwelcome intruder simply for being under a tent. Source: trial testimony reported by Dallas Express, corroborated by CBS Texas live updates, June 6, 2026.

title: Medical examiner: a single wound — not a sustained attack
body: The State's final witness, the Collin County medical examiner, testified that Austin Metcalf died from a SINGLE stab wound. A single motion — not repeated stabbing — is consistent with a split-second reaction in fear, not a sustained assault. The State rested after this testimony, having called roughly 21 witnesses — fewer than the ~35 it had indicated it might call. Source: NBC News, June 6, 2026.

title: Even the State's witness: the push came before the stab
body: Within coverage skeptical of the self-defense claim, the State's own student witness described the sequence plainly: Austin 'leans in to push him, and Karmelo stabs him.' Even in the prosecution's account, the push precedes the response. The order of events is not in dispute. Source: CBS Texas, June 6, 2026.
